title: "Intersight Audit Log and Configuration Changes"
criticality: "high"

---

# UC-19.1.23 · Intersight Audit Log and Configuration Changes

## Description

Detects high-impact configuration and identity changes in Cisco Intersight by searching `cisco:intersight:aaaauditrecord` events (Intersight `aaa.AuditRecord` MO class), coalescing vendor field spellings, applying **severity** from `intersight_high_value_object_types` and **suppression** from `intersight_automation_accounts`, and rolling up to `user_email` + `object_type` + `ObjectMoid` with **Created / Modified / Deleted** event types and `Source` (including Mobile and PowerShell) for triage and compliance evidence.

## Value

You reduce ‘silent’ fleet-wide policy drift: firmware, boot, IAM, and connectivity intent are visible before the next change window, outage, or audit interview. The business gets defensible who-did-what/what-changed records mapped to common frameworks, fewer emergency rollbacks, and a faster bridge between platform engineering and security when someone bypasses the standard console with a mobile or script client. Pairing the rollup with your change calendar tells you whether a burst is a scheduled automation run or a surprise.

## Implementation

Install Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight, ensure `aaa.AuditRecord` (sourcetype `cisco:intersight:aaaauditrecord`) lands in `cisco_intersight`, deploy the two lookup CSVs and `transforms.conf`, then schedule the `spl` as a report. Add alerts for `severity_tier` critical and warning with 1h throttle on `user_email`+`object_type` and a drilldown to `Request`/`Response` JSON on `TraceId`.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• `Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight` ([Splunkbase 5953](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/5953)) on a Heavy Forwarder (or any node licensed for modular inputs) and a matching copy on Search Heads for `fieldsummary` and lookup authoring.
• An Intersight API key (KeyId + Secret) with **Audit** visibility to `GET /api/v1/aaa/AuditRecords` — typical combined roles: **Account Administrator** (read org inventory) + **Audit Administrator** (read audit) per Cisco’s current RBAC labels; your security team’s naming may differ, but the credential must be allowed to list AuditRecords.
• A destination index (commonly `cisco_intersight`) with retention that meets your control framework (90–365+ days is common for privileged-change evidence).
• Outbound HTTPS to the hostname your subscription documents: Cisco Intersight SaaS, Customer Virtual Appliance (CVA) private cloud connector, or Provider Virtual Appliance (PVA) partner-hosted Intersight — not necessarily `intersight.com` in every air-gapped path; mirror the value shown in the Intersight UI’s API endpoint banner.
• Two lookup files in a customer-owned app (example app id `intersight_audit_governance`) — see Step 2 for headers and `transforms.conf`.

**Step 1 — Identify the risk and the evidence requirement (control framing)**

Cisco Intersight is the policy-of-record for a growing share of Cisco UCS, X-Series, stand-alone C-Series, and claimed HyperFlex control-plane objects. A single edit to a service profile, firmware policy, or LAN/SAN connectivity policy can fan out to hundreds of blades after the next service-profile association or the next policy-driven remediation cycle. Unlike host-only hardening, Intersight changes are *infrastructure intent*: they are what the estate is told to become.

**Why the audit log matters to security and resiliency**
• **Identity plane (`iam.*`)** — Weakening an LDAP policy, permission, privilege set, or API key can disable multi-factor requirements, open privilege creep, or allow token replay. These changes often precede data-exfil or lateral movement using management-plane access rather than end-user logins.
• **Boot and BIOS (`boot.*`, `bios.*`)** — A precision boot policy that forces UEFI or SAN-first paths can make large fleets unbootable on the *next* reboot. BIOS policy drift can re-open side-channel mitigations, reset VT-x defaults, or align with a malicious golden image. These are not “someday” risks; they are fleet-wide reliability incidents waiting for the next power event.
• **Network identity (`vnic.*` policies)** — MAC/VLAN/WWPN changes change where traffic lands; misconfiguration can look like a subtle outage in apps that ride those vNICs.
• **Observability plane (`ntp.*`, `snmp.*`, `syslog.*`)** — NTP, SNMP, and syslog **policy** tampering can desynchronise forensics, hide traps, or ship logs to an untrusted index — undermining the very audit you are trying to build.
• **Service profiles (`server.*`)** — The bridge between abstractions and hardware; profile/template edits are standard change activity but must be *expected* in windows or automation accounts.

**Compliance mapping (illustrative — map phrases to *your* policy pack)**
• **SOC 2 CC6.1** — Logical and physical access controls: show who can change the policies that control access to management interfaces.
• **ISO/IEC 27001 A.12.4.3** — Administrator and operator logs: privileged actions on a central control plane.
• **PCI-DSS 10.2** — Audit trails for individual user access to cardholder data systems — map when Intersight policies gate infrastructure that in-scope systems rely on; pair with app-layer logs, not in isolation.
• **NIST SP 800-53 AU-2 / AU-12** — Event logging and audit review for accounts that can alter audit-relevant resources.

**What you are NOT proving with this use case alone** — This UC is about **Intersight-emitted** `aaa.AuditRecord` rows. It does not replace HSM, database, or app-layer DLP evidence; it is the *management-plane* trail you need when someone asks, “Did our golden profile change before the incident?”


**Step 2 — Implement collection, parsing, and lookups**

**(A) Install the TA** — `splunk install app Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight.tgz` on the forwarder and Search Head (or use **Apps → Find More Apps** in Splunk Cloud). If your organisation vendors a wrapper `bin/intersight_setup.py` (some builds still ship a helper), only run the vendor script you actually have — the canonical method is: install app → **Configuration → Intersight Accounts** in the UI → enable modular input per MO class.

**(B) Enable `aaa.AuditRecord` polling** — In the add-on, enable a modular input that targets managed object class `aaa.AuditRecord`. The public REST path is `GET /api/v1/aaa/AuditRecords`. The TA lowercases the MO class to derive sourcetype `cisco:intersight:aaaauditrecord` (all-a’s prefix — verify in **Settings → Sourcetype** after first poll; do not type a hand-wavy `cisco:intersight:auditRecords` name).

`inputs.conf` (reconcile every key name to `default/inputs.conf` in the exact build you run — stanza name below is a working pattern many teams use):

```ini
# $SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight/local/inputs.conf

[ta_cisco_intersight://aaa_audit]
disabled = 0
account = intersight_prod_account
index = cisco_intersight
interval = 300
# Poll only audit records; mo_class or mo_type spelling follows your exact TA:
mo_class = aaa.AuditRecord
# Some TA builds require endpoint URL override; mirror your Intersight SaaS or appliance hostname
# endpoint = https://www.intersight.com/api/v1
```

Credentials stay out of this file. Use the UI workflow or an encrypted `passwords.conf` stanza so KeyId/Secret are never in Git. Restart `splunkd` on the input node when toggling `disabled`.

`props.conf` for the audit sourcetype (UTF-8 JSON, ISO-8601 `CreateTime` in UTC; merge into your `local/props.conf` in the TA or a thin `intsight_audit_parsing` app that loads after the vendor):

```ini
[cisco:intersight:aaaauditrecord]
KV_MODE = json
SHOULD_LINEMERGE = false
TRUNCATE = 0
TIME_PREFIX = "CreateTime":
TIME_FORMAT = %Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S.%3NZ
MAX_TIMESTAMP_LOOKAHEAD = 35
TZ = UTC
LINE_BREAKER = ([\r\n]+)
description = Intersight aaa.AuditRecord — privilege and configuration change evidence
# Optional: FIELDALIAS for long-standing dashboards (keep coalesce in saved searches until stable)
# FIELDALIAS-email = Email AS user_email
```

`transforms.conf` (customer app `intersight_audit_governance`):

```ini
[intersight_high_value_object_types]
filename = intersight_high_value_object_types.csv
case_sensitive_match = true
min_matches = 1
match_type = WILDCARD(object_type)

[intersight_automation_accounts]
filename = intersight_automation_accounts.csv
min_matches = 1
```

`intersight_high_value_object_types.csv` (header + examples — extend to your estate):

```csv
object_type,severity_tier,runbook_url,on_call_team
iam.User,critical,https://wiki.example.com/rb/iam-guardrails,page-iam@example.com
iam.ApiKey,critical,https://wiki.example.com/rb/apikey-rotation,page-iam@example.com
firmware.Policy,critical,https://wiki.example.com/rb/firmware,platform-infra@example.com
boot.PrecisionPolicy,critical,https://wiki.example.com/rb/boot-safety,platform-infra@example.com
bios.Policy,critical,https://wiki.example.com/rb/bios,platform-infra@example.com
server.Profile,critical,https://wiki.example.com/rb/profiles,ucs-sre@example.com
vnic.LanConnectivityPolicy,warning,https://wiki.example.com/rb/vnic,net-sre@example.com
ntp.Policy,warning,https://wiki.example.com/rb/obs-planes,net-sre@example.com
syslog.Policy,warning,https://wiki.example.com/rb/obs-planes,net-sre@example.com
org.Organization,warning,https://wiki.example.com/rb/tenant-borders,security-arch@example.com
compute.RackUnit,info,,
```

`intersight_automation_accounts.csv` (automation, not ad-hoc humans):

```csv
email,automation_purpose,suppress_alert
automation-terraform@example.com,Golden pipeline apply,true
svc-snow-cmdb@example.com,ServiceNow CMDB enrichment (hourly),true
ansible-tower-aa@example.com,Ansible Tower baseline sync,true
cisco-ssa-nightly@example.com,Scheduled Intersight policy compare,true
sre-bulk-refresh@example.com,Planned policy refresh (exception window),false
```

Set **Permissions** on both CSVs: readable by the role that schedules alerts.

**Primary SPL to save as a report/alert (must match the `spl` field in the UC JSON verbatim):**

```spl
// UC-19.1.23 — Intersight aaa.AuditRecord (MO class aaa.AuditRecord, API /api/v1/aaa/AuditRecords). Sourcetype from TA: cisco:intersight:aaaauditrecord
index=cisco_intersight sourcetype="cisco:intersight:aaaauditrecord" earliest=-24h@h
| eval user_email=coalesce(Email, user_email, email, "")
| eval event_type=coalesce(Event, event, action, "")
| eval object_type=coalesce(ObjectType, object_type, "")
| eval source=coalesce(Source, source, "")
| eval trace_id=coalesce(TraceId, trace_id, "")
| eval object_moid=coalesce(ObjectMoid, object_moid, MoidObject, moid_object, "")
| eval account_name=coalesce('Account.Name', account_name, AccountName, "unknown")
| eval org_name=coalesce('Organization.Name', org_name, OrganizationName, "unknown")
| eval account_moid=coalesce('Account.Moid', account_moid, AccountMoid, "")
| eval org_moid=coalesce('Organization.Moid', organization_moid, OrganizationMoid, "")
| where isnotnull(object_type) AND object_type!="" AND event_type IN ("Created", "Modified", "Deleted")
| lookup intersight_automation_accounts email AS user_email OUTPUT automation_purpose, suppress_alert
| where lower(coalesce(suppress_alert, "false"))!="true"
| lookup intersight_high_value_object_types object_type OUTPUT severity_tier, runbook_url, on_call_team
| eval severity_tier=coalesce(
    severity_tier,
    case(
      match(object_type, "(?i)^iam\\.(User|Permission|PrivilegeSet|ApiKey|IdpReference|LdapPolicy)$"), "critical",
      match(object_type, "(?i)^firmware\\."), "critical",
      match(object_type, "(?i)^boot\\."), "critical",
      match(object_type, "(?i)^bios\\."), "critical",
      match(object_type, "(?i)^server\\.(Profile|ProfileTemplate|ConfigChangeDetail)$"), "critical",
      match(object_type, "(?i)^vnic\\."), "warning",
      match(object_type, "(?i)^(ntp|snmp|syslog|kvm|ipmioverlan|vmedia)\\.Policy$"), "warning",
      match(object_type, "(?i)^org\\.Organization$"), "warning",
      match(object_type, "(?i)^terminal\\.AuditLog$"), "warning",
      1=1, "info")
  )
| eval runbook_url=coalesce(runbook_url, "https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/intersight-audit-triage")
| eval recommended_action=case(
    severity_tier=="critical", "Page platform security: verify change record, compare Request/Response JSON, and confirm rollback or freeze before the next maintenance window",
    severity_tier=="warning", "Notify service owner in business hours; add scrutiny if source is Mobile or Powershell",
    1=1, "Record on compliance dashboard; review in weekly change governance"
  )
| stats count as change_count,
    min(_time) as _first,
    max(_time) as _last,
    values(source) as _sources,
    values(trace_id) as _trace_ids,
    first(account_name) as account_name,
    first(org_name) as org_name,
    first(account_moid) as account_moid,
    first(org_moid) as org_moid
    by user_email, object_type, object_moid, event_type, severity_tier, runbook_url, on_call_team, recommended_action
| eval first_change_utc=strftime(_first, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| eval last_change_utc=strftime(_last, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| eval source=mvindex(_sources,0)
| eval trace_ids=mvjoin(_trace_ids, ", ")
| table account_name, org_name, user_email, source, event_type, object_type, object_moid, change_count, first_change_utc, last_change_utc, severity_tier, recommended_action, runbook_url, trace_ids, on_call_team, account_moid, org_moid
| sort severity_tier, -change_count, user_email
```

How this search behaves — **Stage 0** anchors the index and the real TA sourcetype so you are not glob-searching. **Stage 1** coalesces field spellings: Intersight’s REST JSON uses `Email`, `Event`, `ObjectType`, and `ObjectMoid` per the public schema; the TA may have normalised to snake_case. Never filter `action IN (\"Update\", …)` — Intersight uses **Created, Modified, Deleted** for `Event`. **Stage 2** (`intersight_automation_accounts`) silences *known* service principals so a Terraform plan does not page like a person; raw events remain in the index. **Stage 3** classifies with `intersight_high_value_object_types` and a defensive `case()` for new MOs your CSV has not yet listed. **Stage 4** `stats` rolls up to who changed *what* object (`object_moid`) with how many operations and the trace IDs you need in ServiceNow. **Runbook** URL defaults in-SPL; override per class using the lookup.

**Optional: drilldown to raw JSON (do not use as the paging search until you add rate limits):**

```spl
index=cisco_intersight sourcetype="cisco:intersight:aaaauditrecord" earliest=-24h@h
| eval user_email=coalesce(Email, user_email, email, "")
| search user_email="jdoe@example.com" object_type="boot.PrecisionPolicy"
| table _time, user_email, Event, ObjectType, ObjectMoid, Source, Request, Response, TraceId
```


**Step 3 — Validate polling health, field truth, and suppression**

**(1) Account REST object** — On the Search Head (admin role), verify the input is present and not disabled: `| rest /servicesNS/nobody/Splunk_TA_Cisco_Intersight/data/inputs/ta_cisco_intersight splunk_server=local | table title, disabled, eai:acl.app`. (Path segments vary; some builds expose a shorter REST name — if empty, use **Settings → Data inputs** in the UI and confirm the **aaa.AuditRecord** / audit modular input is green.)

**(2) Ingestion smoke** — `index=cisco_intersight sourcetype="cisco:intersight:aaaauditrecord" earliest=-1h | head 5` should return small JSON events with a parsed `_time` near `CreateTime` (UTC). If `head` is empty, check proxy/TLS, API scope, and that you targeted the org your key can read.

**(3) Field distribution** — `... | eval Event=coalesce(Event, event, action, "")` then `| stats values(Event) as events values(ObjectType) as types values(Source) as sources` to confirm the sets include **Created/Modified/Deleted** and sources like **Intersight**, **IntersightAssist**, **Mobile**, **Powershell**.

**(4) Lookups** — `| inputlookup intersight_high_value_object_types` and `| inputlookup intersight_automation_accounts` return your CSV rows with headers intact.

**(5) End-to-end change test** — In a non-prod org, open **Policies → Boot → Precision Policy** (or any non-production profile object), add a description-only edit, then wait one poll (≤5 min). Run the main SPL with `earliest=-15m` and expect a row for your account with a **critical** or **warning** `severity_tier` as defined by the lookup. Roll back the description change when finished.

**(6) Suppression proof** — Log in with an account listed with `suppress_alert=true`, make a small harmless edit, and confirm the event exists in a raw `search` but does **not** appear at the end of the primary SPL (post-lookup filter).

**(7) Metadata audit** — `| metadata type=sourcetypes index=cisco_intersight | search sourcetype="cisco:intersight:aaaauditrecord" | fieldsummary recentTime` to prove continuous ingest during operational reviews.


**Step 4 — Operationalize**

• **Schedule** — Every **15 minutes**, search window **-60m to now** (one-hour overlap) so a delayed poll still lands inside the next run. Stagger if you have twenty heavy searches hitting the same index.

• **Alerting** — **Critical** `severity_tier` → PagerDuty / primary bridge; **warning** → ticket + Slack; **info** → dashboard-only compliance tile. All actions should embed `runbook_url` and a deep link to your enterprise wiki, not a bare Intersight URL (customer-specific tenancy paths change).

• **Throttling** — 1 result per `user_email` + `object_type` per hour: `| throttle key=user_email+object_type window=3600` as the last pre-action pipeline stage (exact command availability depends on Splunk build — for Cloud, prefer **throttle** in alert settings using `user_email,object_type` as fields).

• **Drilldown** — Analyst clicks a row, passes `TraceId` or (`object_moid`, `_time` window) to a second panel: `| search trace_id=...` over `cisco:intersight:aaaauditrecord` and render `Request` and `Response` as prettified JSON (limit line length) for “what did the API actually post?”

• **Dashboard pack** — (1) Timechart of `change_count` by `object_type` per week, (2) top `user_email` by volume, (3) `stats count by Source` (UI vs `Powershell` vs `Mobile` vs API path when derivable from `Request`), (4) table of new `iam.*` change rows in last 24h.

• **ITSI (optional)** — If you have episodes that relate to the same Intersight org, add an **anonymous** `trace_id` field as a correlation key on **informational** (non-paging) episodes only after you test blast radius; do not make ITSI a substitute for a proper Privileged Access Management ticket.

**Runbook URL placeholder (replace before production):** `https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/intersight-audit-uc-19-1-23`


**Step 5 — Tune (after 2+ weeks in production)**

• **Volume review** — If paging never fires, your automation lookup may be over-broad (humans silenced) or the MO list is too narrow. Reconcile with actual top `ObjectType` values in `| top ObjectType` weekly.

• **Add MOs** — Intersight ships new services (e.g. workload-optimisation or orchestration features) over time. When a new `ObjectType` appears in `top` without a row in `intersight_high_value_object_types`, add it and assign a tier and runbook, then document the change in your service catalogue.

• **Source hygiene** — Review `Source` monthly: elevated rates of `Mobile` or `Powershell` can indicate scripting or mobile-console activity outside standard change tools; pair with your VPN or MDM team before assuming malice, but *do* document “shadow-ops” that bypass the ticketing shell.

• **Null Email rows** — `user_email` null often correlates to automation or workflow-originated changes (`workflow.WorkflowInfo` in related streams) — do not auto-close; add a `NOT user_email="*"` only after you are sure you are not discarding a scheduled attack path.

• **Session correlation (advanced)** — Optional secondary join: `cisco:intersight:iamsession` in the same index for the same 15m window to answer “which interactive session was active *before* a burst of audit rows?” — that is a forensics add-on, not a paging default.

• **CIM** — When your team promotes field aliases, enable acceleration for `Change` and `Authentication` so the `cimSpl` variant becomes cheap; re-run a parallel alert for 30 days to confirm parity.


**Appendix A — Sourcetype family note**

Related audit-adjacent sourcetypes in the same TA (lower-cased managed objects): `cisco:intersight:iamuser`, `cisco:intersight:iamsession`, `cisco:intersight:iampermission`, `cisco:intersight:iamprivilegeset`, `cisco:intersight:iamapikey`, `cisco:intersight:iamoauthtoken`, `cisco:intersight:workflowworkflowinfo`. Use them in enrichment dashboards, not in the primary `aaaauditrecord` alert, unless you have a well-tested join key such as time-bounded `User.Moid` and email.

**Appendix B — PII note**

`Email` and `Request/Response` JSON can contain person-identifiable and infrastructure secrets. Index permissions should follow least privilege; consider field-level redaction in `props`/`SEDCMD` for password-like keys if your DLP programme requires it.

**Appendix C — Deduplication**

In rare TA restarts, a duplicate `TraceId` may be re-fetched. For KPI reporting (not the evidence index) you may add `| dedup TraceId` when reporting monthly counts. Document whether dedup is for metrics only.


## SPL

```spl
// UC-19.1.23 — Intersight aaa.AuditRecord (MO class aaa.AuditRecord, API /api/v1/aaa/AuditRecords). Sourcetype from TA: cisco:intersight:aaaauditrecord
index=cisco_intersight sourcetype="cisco:intersight:aaaauditrecord" earliest=-24h@h
| eval user_email=coalesce(Email, user_email, email, "")
| eval event_type=coalesce(Event, event, action, "")
| eval object_type=coalesce(ObjectType, object_type, "")
| eval source=coalesce(Source, source, "")
| eval trace_id=coalesce(TraceId, trace_id, "")
| eval object_moid=coalesce(ObjectMoid, object_moid, MoidObject, moid_object, "")
| eval account_name=coalesce('Account.Name', account_name, AccountName, "unknown")
| eval org_name=coalesce('Organization.Name', org_name, OrganizationName, "unknown")
| eval account_moid=coalesce('Account.Moid', account_moid, AccountMoid, "")
| eval org_moid=coalesce('Organization.Moid', organization_moid, OrganizationMoid, "")
| where isnotnull(object_type) AND object_type!="" AND event_type IN ("Created", "Modified", "Deleted")
| lookup intersight_automation_accounts email AS user_email OUTPUT automation_purpose, suppress_alert
| where lower(coalesce(suppress_alert, "false"))!="true"
| lookup intersight_high_value_object_types object_type OUTPUT severity_tier, runbook_url, on_call_team
| eval severity_tier=coalesce(
    severity_tier,
    case(
      match(object_type, "(?i)^iam\\.(User|Permission|PrivilegeSet|ApiKey|IdpReference|LdapPolicy)$"), "critical",
      match(object_type, "(?i)^firmware\\."), "critical",
      match(object_type, "(?i)^boot\\."), "critical",
      match(object_type, "(?i)^bios\\."), "critical",
      match(object_type, "(?i)^server\\.(Profile|ProfileTemplate|ConfigChangeDetail)$"), "critical",
      match(object_type, "(?i)^vnic\\."), "warning",
      match(object_type, "(?i)^(ntp|snmp|syslog|kvm|ipmioverlan|vmedia)\\.Policy$"), "warning",
      match(object_type, "(?i)^org\\.Organization$"), "warning",
      match(object_type, "(?i)^terminal\\.AuditLog$"), "warning",
      1=1, "info")
  )
| eval runbook_url=coalesce(runbook_url, "https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/intersight-audit-triage")
| eval recommended_action=case(
    severity_tier=="critical", "Page platform security: verify change record, compare Request/Response JSON, and confirm rollback or freeze before the next maintenance window",
    severity_tier=="warning", "Notify service owner in business hours; add scrutiny if source is Mobile or Powershell",
    1=1, "Record on compliance dashboard; review in weekly change governance"
  )
| stats count as change_count,
    min(_time) as _first,
    max(_time) as _last,
    values(source) as _sources,
    values(trace_id) as _trace_ids,
    first(account_name) as account_name,
    first(org_name) as org_name,
    first(account_moid) as account_moid,
    first(org_moid) as org_moid
    by user_email, object_type, object_moid, event_type, severity_tier, runbook_url, on_call_team, recommended_action
| eval first_change_utc=strftime(_first, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| eval last_change_utc=strftime(_last, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
| eval source=mvindex(_sources,0)
| eval trace_ids=mvjoin(_trace_ids, ", ")
| table account_name, org_name, user_email, source, event_type, object_type, object_moid, change_count, first_change_utc, last_change_utc, severity_tier, recommended_action, runbook_url, trace_ids, on_call_team, account_moid, org_moid
| sort severity_tier, -change_count, user_email
```

## CIM SPL

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=true count from datamodel=Change where nodename=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.user All_Changes.object
| head 20
```

## Visualization

Sortable table of rollups (see `table` in SPL); timechart of change_count by `object_type`; bar chart of `source` (UI vs Mobile vs PowerShell); single-value tiles for new `iam.*` in 24h; second drilldown panel with prettified `Request`/`Response` for one `TraceId`.
